lesson.menu_desc: Open Block 6: give and take feedback in a performance review. Structure feedback with the STAR method (Situation, Task, Action, Result), balance praise with a development area, and report what was said with reported speech — backshifting tense and shifting pronouns and time words ("she said the report had been late", "he told me he would fix it"). New vocab: appraisal, feedback, praise, recognition, strengths, development area, accomplishment, underdeliver, actionable, follow-through, raise the bar, commendable — 33 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 19 — Feedback & Performance Reviews
lesson.title_tag: Professor View — Victor Malvezi Paschotto | Aula 19 — Feedback & Performance Reviews

listenings.text: Prep note to myself, the night before I run mid-year reviews for my team at Brink's. Three conversations tomorrow, and I want to get the structure right this time, because last cycle I rambled and people left unsure of where they stood. So here is my plan. For each person, I open with genuine recognition, something specific they did well, not a vague nice job. Then I use the STAR method to keep it concrete: I name the situation, the task they owned, the action they took, and the result it produced. That stops feedback from turning into opinion. Then, and this is the part I usually rush, I name one development area honestly, framed as actionable, with a clear follow-through we agree on together. With Sofia, the strength is obvious, her client work has been commendable, but she underdelivered on the quarterly dashboard and I need to say so without crushing her. With the second analyst, the praise is the easy part and the development area is time management. And with the third, I want to raise the bar on his presentations, because he is ready for more. One more thing I keep forgetting: a review is a two-way street. I should ask each of them for feedback on how I lead, and actually listen instead of getting defensive. If I balance the praise with one honest development area, keep it specific with STAR, and leave each person with a clear next step, then tomorrow goes well. Sleep now, lead well in the morning.

Hi Victor, it's Clara, your coach, with a quick voice note before your review cycle tomorrow. I have three things for you, and they all come down to landing feedback that people can actually use. First, lead with recognition that is specific. Telling someone their work was commendable means nothing on its own. Tell them what they did and what it produced. That is the whole point of the STAR method, and you already know it: situation, task, action, result. When you anchor praise in a result, it sticks, and when you anchor a development area in a result, it stops feeling personal. Second, do not bury the one thing that has to change. If someone underdelivered, name it, but make it actionable, with a follow-through you both own. Vague feedback is unkind feedback, because the person cannot act on it. Third, and this is the one people forget, when you carry feedback up the chain to your director, you will be reporting what your team said, so watch your grammar. If Sofia tells you today, I am stretched too thin, then in the review tomorrow you report it as she said she was stretched too thin. The tense steps back, the pronoun shifts, and the time word moves: today becomes that day, will becomes would, can becomes could. Get that right and you sound precise instead of sloppy. Balance the praise, make the development area actionable, and report accurately. You have got this. Go raise the bar.
